breakClass: GroupBy-then-Select(g => g.OrderBy*/FirstOrDefault()) returning an ENTITY/row, used server-side (JOINed on g.FirstOrDefault(), or composed as a correlated 'latest-per-key' subquery in a non-materialized IQueryable). EF Core 10 cannot translate the grouped FirstOrDefault outside an aggregate context -> "could not be translated" runtime 500. Every finding has the identical shape in _archive/legacy-mvc5 where EF6 translated it, so each is a port regression.
why: amcremarsk is an IQueryable: GroupBy(TransactionId).Select(g => g.OrderByDescending(CreatedDate).FirstOrDefault()) (latest remark per TransactionId). NOT materialized, then JOINed into rem ('join d in amcremarsk on a.AmcId equals d.TransactionId'), with rem enumerated server-side at .Distinct().ToList(). EF Core 10 cannot translate a GroupBy().Select(g => g.OrderByDescending().FirstOrDefault()) subquery used as a join source -> 500. Med (not high): runs only inside the if(1==1) 12-hour-followup branch of the AddRemark POST after SaveChanges, fires when a user adds an AMC remark, not on a hot grid. Legacy AMCController.cs:4079/4085 has identical GroupBy + 'join d in amcremarsk' => port regression.
finalFix:
Mirror the team's established .AsEnumerable() remediation by forcing the latest-per-TransactionId map to evaluate client-side BEFORE it is used as a join source, then join the in-memory list. This preserves exact semantics ('latest amcremarks remark per TransactionId, with nexttime defaulted to Now+2yr when null, kept only if within 12h').

Replace lines 4144-4177 with:

  var dt = System.DateTime.Now.AddYears(2);
  var amcremarsk = (from a in db.AddedRemarks
                    where a.TransactionType == "amcremarks"
                    select new { a.TransactionId, a.CreatedDate, a.nexttime })
                   .AsEnumerable()                              // materialize before grouped FirstOrDefault
                   .GroupBy(r => r.TransactionId)
                   .Select(g => g.OrderByDescending(r => r.CreatedDate).First())  // group never empty
                   .Select(r => new { r.TransactionId, r.CreatedDate, nexttime = r.nexttime ?? dt })
                   .ToList();

  var curdate = System.DateTime.Now;
  // window filter done in memory to mirror DateDiffHour(curdate, nexttime) < 12
  var amcMap = amcremarsk
      .Where(d => (d.nexttime - curdate).TotalHours < 12)
      .ToDictionary(d => d.TransactionId, d => d.nexttime);
  var amcIds = amcMap.Keys.ToList();

  var rem = (from a in db.Amcs
             join b in db.AmcAssignedTos on a.AmcId equals b.AmcId
             join c in db.Customers on a.CustomerId equals c.CustomerID
             where amcIds.Contains(a.AmcId)
                && b.Status == "Assigned" && b.ChkStatus == Status.active
             select new { b.EmployeeId, a.AmcId, taskname = a.AmcNo + "-" + c.CustomerName })
            .Distinct().ToList()
            .Select(x => new { x.EmployeeId, x.AmcId, nexttime = amcMap[x.AmcId], curdate, x.taskname })
            .ToList();

Notes:
- The original DateDiffHour(curdate, nexttime) computes whole-hour difference of (nexttime - curdate); using (d.nexttime - curdate).TotalHours < 12 is the in-memory equivalent of the legacy '< 12' window. If you need to match SQL DATEDIFF(hour,...) truncation exactly, use (int)System.Math.Floor((d.nexttime - curdate).TotalHours) or compute hour-boundary difference; the simple TotalHours form preserves the intended 'within 12h' behavior.
- Downstream code (pids/foreach at lines 4179+) only reads AmcId, taskname, EmployeeId, so the reshaped 'rem' is drop-in compatible; nexttime/curdate are retained in the projection to preserve the original anonymous-shape.
- Alternative (smaller diff, same effect): keep the original 'rem' LINQ but make amcremarsk a materialized List by adding .AsEnumerable() before .GroupBy(...).Select(g => g.OrderByDescending(...).First()).ToList(), and replace the EF.Functions.DateDiffHour filter + the join-on-IQueryable with the in-memory amcMap/amcIds approach above (the join cannot stay against db.* once amcremarsk is a List unless you switch the in-memory list to the join's outer side, so the amcIds.Contains form is preferred).
reasoning:
I could NOT refute the claim; independent inspection confirms it. FILE: Controllers/AMCController.cs, method AddRemark(AddedRemarksvm amcremarks), inside the if(1==1) 12-hour-followup branch.

(a) SERVER-SIDE: confirmed. amcremarsk (lines 4144-4157) is an IQueryable rooted in db.AddedRemarks (DbSet<AddedRemarks>, ApplicationDbContext.cs:65). There is NO .ToList()/.AsEnumerable()/.AsArray() boundary on it â€” it terminates at .GroupBy(r => r.TransactionId).Select(g => g.OrderByDescending(r => r.CreatedDate).FirstOrDefault()) (line 4156-4157). It is then consumed as a JOIN SOURCE in rem: `join d in amcremarsk on a.AmcId equals d.TransactionId` (line 4162). rem is a single composed IQueryable over db.Amcs/db.AmcAssignedTos/amcremarsk/db.Customers with a server-side WHERE EF.Functions.DateDiffHour(curdate, d.nexttime) < 12 (line 4164) and a projection referencing d.nexttime (line 4173), enumerated server-side only at .Distinct().ToList() (line 4177). So amcremarsk is translated to SQL as a correlated/derived subquery. This is NOT a group-aggregate over a non-empty IGrouping (the Select returns the full anonymous row {TransactionId, CreatedDate, nexttime}, not the key, not a scalar aggregate), NOT a standalone top-level query, and NOT after a materialization boundary.

(b) THROWS 500 in EF Core 10: confirmed. The shape GroupBy(key).Select(g => g.OrderByDescending(x).FirstOrDefault()) returning a non-key entity/row used as a join source is the canonical EF Core 'grouped FirstOrDefault outside an aggregate context' that EF Core 10 cannot translate -> InvalidOperationException 'could not be translated' = HTTP 500. Decisive corroboration: the team's OWN established remediation for this identical shape is in the live tree â€” ~20 sites in MyReportsController.cs (e.g. lines 10552, 10588, 10624...11205) and operationprocedureController.cs:601 already carry `.AsEnumerable().GroupBy(l => l.ApprovedBy).Select(g => g.OrderByDescending(c => c.CreatedDate).FirstOrDefault())`. The AMC site is the same shape but UN-fixed (no .AsEnumerable()), and is worse because the grouped-FirstOrDefault is a JOIN source rather than a `let` subquery, forcing a derived-table translation EF Core 10 rejects.

PORT REGRESSION: confirmed. _archive/legacy-mvc5/Controllers/AMCController.cs lines 4067-4101 are structurally identical (same GroupBy+Select+`join d in amcremarsk`), differing only by DbFunctions.DiffHours -> EF.Functions.DateDiffHour. EF6 translated/ran it fine; EF Core 10 does not.

SEVERITY: med. It runs only in the if(1==1) 12-hour AMC-followup branch of the AddRemark POST after SaveChanges (fires when a user adds an AMC remark, not on a hot grid), but it WILL throw deterministically on that path once AddedRemarks has amcremarks rows â€” so on the realistic AddRemark flow it is effectively a guaranteed 500.

NOTE on entity types: AddedRemarks.CreatedDate is non-nullable DateTime and AddedRemarks.nexttime is DateTime? (MaterialRequisition.cs:115-126); the nexttime null-coalesce to dt is fine, the break is purely the grouped-FirstOrDefault-as-join-source translation, not a datetime overflow.
